modules.title: Module 10: Vital Signs
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to prepare students to know how, when and why vital signs are taken and how to report and chart these procedures. Students will learn the correct procedure for measuring temperature, pulse, respirations, and blood pressure. They will learn to recognize and report normal and abnormal findings.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Describe what is meant by vital signs, their purpose, and observations made while performing the procedures
Discuss the use of temperature as an indicator of body function
Describe nursing measures to raise and lower the temperature of the body
Describe the circulatory system as it relates to pulse, and identify the pulse sites
Describe factors that increase and decrease pulse, and the qualities to observe in taking a pulse
Define and describe respiration and factors that affect respiratory rate
Describe observations to be made when measuring respirations
Describe abnormal breathing patterns
Describe the process for taking TPR as a combined procedure
Describe what happens in the circulatory system to produce blood pressure
Identify factors that increase or decrease blood pressure
Identify parts of the blood pressure equipment
Discuss the procedure for taking a blood pressure reading
Discuss observation and reporting of patient’s/resident’s pain
Record vital signs on chart, graph, and Nursing Assistant notes

modules.title: Module 11: Nutrition
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to examine the body’s need for food and the effect of food on the body. This module includes the basic food groups, nutrients, and common therapeutic diets, as well as ways to assist a patient/resident to meet nutrition and hydration needs.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Discuss the body’s need for food and fluids
List common nutrients and their food sources
Describe the My Pyramid food guidance system
Describe the vegan basic four food groups
Discuss nutritional and fluid needs of the elderly
Describe therapeutic diets commonly ordered for patients/residents and the responsibilities of the Nurse Assistant
Describe proper techniques for feeding patients/residents
Discuss cultural and religious influences on dietary practices
Identify alternative ways to administer nutrition

modules.title: Module 12: Emergency Procedures
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to introduce the student to the concepts and procedures related to emergency procedures, signs and symptoms of distress, and the role of the Nurse Assistant in Long Term Care (LTC) in the response to immediate and temporary intervention in emergency situations.

objectives.text: Define key terminology.
Identify common signs and symptoms of conditions associated with patient/resident distress and describe the Nurse Assistant’s role and responsibility in preventing and/or responding.
Describe the immediate interventions in a medical emergency.
List the causes and signs of choking and discuss the use of abdominal thrusts for relief of obstructed airway.
Describe common emergency codes used in long-term care facilities. This module may be taught concurrently with Module 4: Safe Environment

modules.title: Module 13: Long Term Care Resident
modules.theory_hours: 5 SNF/ICF (or 3 non-SNF/ICF plus 2 additional training hours)
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to introduce the student to the basic structure of the body and to review the effect of aging on body structure and function. Common physical and psychological conditions found in elderly patients are presented along with approaches to care. Community resources commonly available to assist elderly patients with their psychological, recreational, and social needs are presented.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Describe common basic human needs and interventions for the elderly patient/resident; environmental, psychological, social, recreational and spiritual
Describe common community resources to meet the needs of the elderly
Describe the special needs of persons with developmental and mental disorders including intellectual disability, cerebral palsy, epilepsy, Parkinson’s disease, and mental illness
Describe the special needs of persons with Alzheimer’s Disease and other related dementias
Describe the body’s basic organization and composition

List the body systems, including basic anatomy and physiology, common diseases of the elderly with signs and symptoms, Nurse Assistant duties and observations, aging changes and complications of immobility

Describe changes in body systems associated with aging Refer to CDPH 276C form: *SNF/ICF providers need to complete A1 Special needs of persons with developmental and mental disorders including intellectual disability, cerebral palsy, epilepsy, Parkinson’s disease, and mental illness (2 hours); A2 Special needs of persons with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (2 hours); and B Introduction to anatomy and physiology (B-F minimum 1 hour). **Non-SNF/ICF providers may complete A2 Special needs of persons with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (2 hours), or two (2) hours of training from another module in order to meet the sixty (60) hours minimum theory training requirement and B Introduction to anatomy and physiology (B-F minimum 1 hour).

modules.title: Module 14- Rehabilitative Nursing
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to introduce the Nurse Assistant to restorative care. Each individual is entitled to reach his/her optimal level of functioning. The Nurse Assistant assists the patient/resident in achieving maximum independent living skills through use of rehabilitative or restorative procedures.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Discuss rehabilitation (restorative care) and how it promotes independence and patient/resident potential
State goals of restorative care that promote independence and patient/resident potential
Describe the rehabilitation team, state its purpose, and discuss the role of the Nurse Assistant as a member of the team
List the responsibilities of the Nurse Assistant in promoting patient/resident self-care
List activities that make up Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
List common comfort and adaptive devices and explain the purposes of each
Identify steps to prevent complications from inactivity
Describe range-of-motion exercises (ROM)
Identify procedures and devices used to promote mobility and ambulation for patients/residents with physical and/or visual impairment
Discuss relationship between patient’s/resident’s self-esteem and family involvement in care
Discuss the process of documentation and the Nurse Assistant role in care plan meeting

modules.title: Module 15: Observation and Charting
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to prepare students to know how, when, and why to use objective and subjective observation skills. They will report and record observations on appropriate documents using medical terms and abbreviations.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Identify word elements used in medical terms
Identify medical terminology and abbreviations commonly used in medical facilities
Define observation and list the senses used to observe a patient/resident.
Describe objective and subjective observations
List types of charting documents and the use for each
Explain how to accurately complete ADL assessment for MDS
Discuss procedures to use when recording on a patient’s/resident’s chart

modules.title: Module 16: Death and Dying
modules.statement_of_purpose: The Nurse Assistant is introduced to the various stages of the grieving process and physical signs of approaching death. This unit introduces death as a normal stage of life. The health care provider must recognize the physical, psychological, and spiritual needs of the patient/resident during this period to understand coping mechanisms and provide support to the patient/resident and family members.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Describe the five stages of the grieving process according to Dr. Kubler-Ross
Describe approaches to meet common emotional and spiritual needs of terminally ill patients/residents and their families
List the rights of the dying patient/resident
Differentiate between common signs of approaching death and biological death
Identify care and comfort measures for dying patients/residents
Identify the philosophy and goals of hospice and the Nurse Assistant’s role when the patient/resident is cared for by a licensed hospice nurse
Describe procedures and responsibilities for postmortem care

modules.title: Module 17: Patient/Resident Abuse
modules.statement_of_purpose: The purpose of this unit is to introduce the Nurse Assistant to patient/resident abuse. The module will focus on the nurse assistant role in preventing, recognizing, and reporting instances of patient/resident abuse.

objectives.text: Define key terminology
Identify and discuss types of elder abuse
Discuss the issues related to elder abuse
Explain the Nurse Assistant role in preventing elder abuse
Describe the Nurse Assistant role in reporting elder abuse
